narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 58.5% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including being in the 2nd decile for Year 1 grades and the 1st decile for Year 3 grades—both well below the typical student's 6th or 5th decile rankings—along with your LSAT score of 25.5, undergraduate GPA of 3.0, middle family income bracket, Hispanic background, female gender, and plan to attend full-time. Among these, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence, pushing the model firmly toward predicting failure since it's so much lower than what succeeds for most students. Your LSAT score came in second, as its below-average level compared to the usual 36.7 further reinforced concerns about bar exam readiness. Your Year 1 decile ranked third in impact, similarly highlighting early academic struggles that deviate from the norm. Factors like your family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender followed with progressively smaller influences, all nudging the prediction toward failure in your specific case, though they're closer to typical distributions. Overall, your profile stands out from the average applicant, who tends to have stronger high school deciles, higher LSATs, and slightly better GPAs, often from White or male backgrounds in higher income brackets. This combination made the model view your bar passage odds as riskier than for peers who get approved. The good news is some of these are changeable: retaking the LSAT to aim above 36 could dramatically shift future predictions, as it's a top influencer. Focusing on boosting your GPA through stronger undergrad performance or targeted prep might help too, since academic deciles weigh heavily. Even family income isn't set in stone—scholarships or support programs could elevate that aspect over time. I encourage you to seek bar prep resources early, perhaps starting with practice exams mirroring the LSAT's demands, to build the skills that sway these models. With targeted improvements, you could reapply stronger and turn this around. Keep pushing—you've got potential here.
